content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 28, 2016

Janet E. Coleson
Town of Fairfax Attorney
Best Best & Krieger, LLP
2001 N. Main Street, Suite 390
Walnut Creek, CA 94596

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-16-134

Dear Ms. Coleson:

        This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

        Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

        Does Council Member Coler have a conflict of interest in participating in a decision
involving the proposed rezoning and development of the 2-acre parcel as lower income senior
housing because of the proximity of her real property to the proposed project?

CONCLUSION

        Yes. It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
Council Member Coler's single family residence, which is located within 500 feet of the 2-acre
parcel subject to the rezoning.

FACTS

        You are the Town Attorney for the Town of Fairfax and your write on behalf of Council
Member Barbara Coler. Fairfax covers 2.2 square miles in Marin and has a population of 7,607
(2013).

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


If there should be text in this document, please try:
- Re-uploading the image
- Checking that the image file is correct
- Ensuring the document is readable and not overexposed

I'm ready to transcribe once a legible version is available.

File No. A-16-134
Page No. 2

The Town has received an application for development, including a rezoning, of property
located at 2626 Sir Francis Drake Boulevard. The property is 20-acres in size, and is commonly
referred to as the "Lutheran Church site." The project would divide the 20-acres into two lots,
comprised of one 2-acre lot and one 18-acre lot. The 2-acre lot would be rezoned to PDD (Planned
Development District), and the 18-acre lot would remain UR-7 (Upland Residential Zone, seven-
acre minimum parcel size). The 2-acre lot is under 500 feet from Council Member Coler's
townhouse.

The new zoning would permit lower income senior housing, at a density of not less than 20
units per acre, to be located on the 2-acre parcel. The applicant is requesting a density bonus, for a
total of 54 units. The proposal also includes 39 parking spaces. The new building will be
approximately 50,755 square feet, constructed in an "E" shape, with two and three story wings that
wrap around two courtyards. The subject 2-acre lot had previously been used for a church and a
private school. The church has declining membership and plans to close in the near future. The
school, which is no longer operating, served grades K-8, and enrolled 73-78 students.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in
making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest." (Section
87100.) A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87103
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or
on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103.)

Financial Interest

The interest here is real property in which Council Member Coler has an interest of $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

Foreseeability and Materiality

A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved in
a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named party
in, or subject of, a government decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) Council Member Coler's financial
interest is not explicitly involved in a decision on the 2-acre lot. For interests that are not explicitly
involved, as here, the effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. "In general,
if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." (Regulation 18701(b).)

For real property not explicitly involved in a decision and pertinent to your facts, a
foreseeable effect is material if the decision:

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, private, noise


File No. A-16-134
Page No. 3

levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial
interest;

"(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property . . .
Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice
allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if the Commission
determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property;

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the
market value of the official's property." (Regulation 18702.2(a)(10) and (12).)

Based on the facts provided, it would appear that a governmental decision concerning the
proposed rezoning and application for development of 54 housing units in a building with two and
three story wings on a parcel that is currently used for a church and includes a vacated school could
change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering intensity of use,
including parking, of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, or
noise levels, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in
which the official has a financial interest. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(10).) Moreover, owning
residential property in such close proximity to the 2-acre parcel subject to the application would
cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the circumstances, to
believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect
would influence the market value of your real property. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(12).) Thus, the
effect of the decision will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on Council Member
Color's interest and Council Member Coler may not make, participate in making, or influence the
decision.²

Public Generally Exception

Regulation 18703 provides:

"(a) General Rule. A governmental decision's financial effect on a public
official's financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public
generally if the official establishes that a significant segment of the public is
affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to
the effect on the significant segment."

___________________________
². When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (such as a city council member) has a
conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of
the item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic
interest on the record of the meeting, (2) recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the
discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105. Regulation 18707.)

I'm looking at what appears to be a blank or nearly blank page. The image shows a white/light gray background with no clearly visible text or content to transcribe.

If there is text present in this document, it may be too faint for me to read reliably. Could you please verify that the image uploaded correctly, or provide a clearer version of the document?

File No. A-16-134
Page No. 4

A significant segment of the public (as pertinent to your facts) is at least 25 percent of:

"(2) All real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the official's jurisdiction"

In this case, Fairfax is a relatively small jurisdiction and it is unclear whether the decision may affect 25% of the entire jurisdiction. To the extent that at least 25% of the jurisdiction are affected in some way, Council Member Coler's interest in her townhome would likely be affected uniquely considering the nature of this particular project including her property's close proximity to the project site and the magnitude of the proposed development. Thus, we conclude the public generally exception would not apply.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:jgl

Does Council Member Coler have a conflict of interest in participating in a decision 
involving the proposed rezoning and development of the 2-acre parcel as lower income senior 
housing because of the proximity of her real property to the proposed project?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
Council Member Coler's single family residence, which is located within 500 feet of the 2-acre 
parcel subject to the rezoning.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Council Member Coler have a conflict of interest in participating in a decision 
involving the proposed rezoning and development of the 2-acre parcel as lower income senior 
housing because of the proximity of her real property to the proposed project?

CONCLUSION: Yes. It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
Council Member Coler's single family residence, which is located within 500 feet of the 2-acre 
parcel subject to the rezoning.
